records.ety: [OE. studie, L. studium, akin to studere to study; possibly akin to Gr. <?/ haste, zeal, <?/ to hasten; cf. OF. estudie, estude, F. tude. Cf. Etude, Student, Studio, Study, v. i.]

defs: A setting of the mind or thoughts upon a subject; hence, application of mind to books, arts, or science, or to any subject, for the purpose of acquiring knowledge.
quotes: Hammond . . . spent thirteen hours of the day in study.
Study gives strength to the mind; conversation, grace.

defs: Mental occupation; absorbed or thoughtful attention; meditation; contemplation.
quotes: Just men they seemed, and all their study bent To worship God aright, and know his works.

defs: Any particular branch of learning that is studied; any object of attentive consideration.
quotes: The Holy Scriptures, especially the New Testament, are her daily study.
The proper study of mankind is man.

defs: A building or apartment devoted to study or to literary work.

A representation or rendering of any object or scene intended, not for exhibition as an original work of art, but for the information, instruction, or assistance of the maker; as, a study of heads or of hands for a figure picture.
A piece for special practice. See Etude.

records.ety: [OE. studien, OF. estudier, F. tudier. See Study, n.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Studied (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Studying (?).]

defs: To fix the mind closely upon a subject; to dwell upon anything in thought; to muse; to ponder.
quotes: I found a moral first, and then studied for a fable.

defs: To apply the mind to books or learning.
To endeavor diligently; to be zealous.
To apply the mind to; to read and examine for the purpose of learning and understanding; as, to study law or theology; to study languages.
To consider attentively; to examine closely; as, to study the work of nature.
quotes: Study thyself; what rank or what degree The wise Creator has ordained for thee.

defs: To form or arrange by previous thought; to con over, as in committing to memory; as, to study a speech.
To make an object of study; to aim at sedulously; to devote one's thoughts to; as, to study the welfare of others; to study variety in composition.
